pages.title: Algebraic geometry and formal geometry
pages.html:
IX Algebraic geometry and formal geometry

The aim of this exposé is to generalize, to the case of a morphism which is not proper, theorems 3.4.2 and 4.1.5 of EGA III.

blocks.label: IX
blocks.html: IX Algebraic geometry and formal geometry
The aim of this exposé is to generalize, to the case of a morphism which is not proper, theorems 3.4.2 and 4.1.5 of EGA III.

pages.title: The comparison theorem
pages.html:
1 The comparison theorem

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) be a morphism of preschemes, separated and of finite type. Suppose that \(X'\) is locally noetherian. Let \(Y'\) be a closed subset of \(X'\) and let \(Y\subset f^{-1}(Y')\). Let \(\widehat{X}\) and \(\widehat{X'}\) be the formal completions of \(X\) and of \(X'\) along \(Y\) and \(Y'\). Let \(\widehat{f}\) be the morphism deduced from \(f\) by passage to the completions.

\[ \tag{1.1} \xymatrix{ X \ar[d]_f & Y \ar[l] \ar[d]& &\widehat{X}\ar[d]_{\widehat{f}} \ar[r]^j & X\ar[d]^f &\\ X^{\prime} & Y^{\prime} \ar[l] &, &\widehat{X^{\prime}} \ar[r]^i & X^{\prime}. } \]

We denote by \(j\) (resp. \(i\)) the homomorphism from \(\widehat{X}\) into \(X\) (resp. from \(\widehat{X'}\) into \(X'\)). It is known that \(i\) and \(j\) are flat.

Let \(\mathscr{I} '\) be an ideal of definition of \(Y'\) and let \({\mathscr{J}}=f^{*}(\mathscr{I} '). \mathscr{O}_X\), it is an ideal of definition of \(Y\). We thus have:

\[ \tag{1.2} {\widehat{X'}=(Y', \varprojlim_{k \in \mathbf{N}}\mathscr{O}_{X'}/{{\mathscr{I}'}^{k+1}})}, \quad {\widehat{X}=(Y, \varprojlim_{k \in \mathbf{N}}\mathscr{O}_X/{{\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}})}. \]

For every \(k \in \mathbf{N}\), set:

\[ \tag{1.3} Y'_k=(Y', \mathscr{O}_{X'}/{{\mathscr{I}'}^{k+1}}), \quad {Y_k}=(Y, \mathscr{O}_X/{{\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}}). \]

Let \(F\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. For every \(k \in \mathbf{N}\), one sets:

\[ \tag{1.4} F_k=F/{\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}F, \qquad \widehat{F}=j^*(F)\simeq \varprojlim F_k. \]

If one sets:

\[ \tag{1.5} R^i f_*(F)^{\wedge}=\varprojlim_{k \in \mathbf{N}} (R^i f_*(F)^{\wedge} \otimes_{{\mathscr{O}}_{X'}}{\mathscr{O}}_{Y'_k}), \quad i \in \mathbf{Z}, \]

one has a natural homomorphism:

\[ \tag{1.6} r_i: i^*(R^if_*(F)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R^i f_*(F)^{\wedge}, \]

which is an isomorphism when \( R^i f_*(F)\) is coherent.

As is explained in EGA III 4.1.1, one has a commutative diagram:

\[ \tag{1.7} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{ i^*(R^i f_*(F)) \ar[rr]^-{\rho_i} \ar[d]^{r_i} & &R^i\widehat{f}_*(\widehat{F}) \ar[d]^{\psi_i} \\ R^i f_*(F)^{\wedge}\ar[rr]^-{\varphi_i} & &\varprojlim_{k \in \mathbf{N}} R^i f_*(F_k). } \end{array} \]

In loc. cit. one finds a commutative diagram, for it is known that \( R^if_*(F)\) is coherent, and one identifies the source and the target of (1.6). In our case, \( R^if_*(F)\) will be coherent only for certain values of \(i\), for which one will study (1.7).

Consider the graded ring

\[ \tag{1.8} \mathscr{S} = \coprod_{k \in \mathbf{N}} \mathscr{I}'^k, \]

and the graded \(\mathscr{S}\)-Module:

\[ \tag{1.9} \mathscr{H}^i= \coprod_{k \in \mathbf{N}} R^if_*({\mathscr{J}}^k F), \quad i \in \mathbf{Z}, \]

whose structure of \(\mathscr{S}\)-Module is defined as follows.

The sheaf \(R^if_*({\mathscr{J}}^k F)\) is associated with the presheaf which, to every affine open set \(U\) of \(X'\), associates:

\[ \tag{1.10} H^i(f^{-1}(U), {\mathscr{J}}^k F{|f^{-1}(U)}). \]

So let \(U'\) be an affine open set of \(X'\), set

\[ U=f^{-1}(U'), \]

and let \(x\in {\mathscr{I}}^m(U')\). Let \(x'\) be the image of \(x\) in \({\mathscr{J}}^{{k}} (U)\). The homothety of ratio \(x'\) in \(F_{|U'}\) maps \({\mathscr{J}}^kF{{|U'}}\) into \({\mathscr{J}}^{k+m}F{{|U'}}\), whence, by functoriality, a morphism:

\[ \tag{1.11} \mu^i_{x, k}(U):H^i(U', {\mathscr{J}}^kF{{|U'}})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i(U', {\mathscr{J}}^{k+m}F{{|U'}}), \]

defined for every \(i \in \mathbf{Z}\) and every \(k \in \mathbf{N}\), which gives, by passage to the associated sheaf, the structure of graded \(\mathscr{S}\)-Module of \(\mathscr{H}^i\).

Theorem 1.1

Let \(n\) be an integer. Suppose that the graded \(\mathscr{S}\)-Module \(\mathscr{H}^i\) is of finite type for \(i=n-1\) and \(i=n\), then:

  1. \(r_n\) and \(r_{n-1}\) are isomorphisms and \(R^i\widehat{f}_*(\widehat{F})\) is coherent for \(i=n-1\);

  2. for \(i=n-1\), \(\rho_i\), \(\varphi_i\) and \(\psi_i\) are topological isomorphisms (in particular the filtration defined on \(R^{n-1}f_*(F)\) by the kernels of the homomorphisms

    \[ \tag{1.12} R^{n-1}f_*(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R^{n-1}f_*(F_k) \]

    is \(\mathscr{I}'\)-good);

  3. for \(i=n\), \(\rho_i\), \(\varphi_i\) and \(\psi_i\) are monomorphisms, moreover the filtration on \(R^n f_*(F)\) is \(\mathscr{I}'\)-good and \(\psi_n\) is an isomorphism;

  4. The projective system of the \(R^i f_*(F_k)\) satisfies, for \(i=n-2, n-1\), the uniform Mittag-Leffler condition, i.e. there exists a fixed integer \(k\geqslant 0\) such that, for every \(p\geqslant 0\) and every \(p'\geqslant p+k\), one has:

    \[ \operatorname{Im}[R^i f_*(F_{p'})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R^i f_*(F_p)]=\operatorname{Im}[R^i f_*(F_{p+k})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R^i f_*(F_p)]. \]

By proceeding as in EGA III 4.1.8, it is easy to reduce to the case where \(X'\) is the spectrum of a noetherian ring \(A\). In this case, it is known that

\[ \tag{1.13} R^if_*(F)=H^i(X, F)^\thicksim \quad \text{(cf. 1.10).} \]

Let \(I\) be the ideal of \(A\) such that \(\widetilde{I}=\mathscr{I}'\) and let

\[ \tag{1.14} S=\mathop{\textstyle\bigoplus}\limits_{k \in \mathbf{N}}I^k, \]
\[ \tag{1.15} H^i=\mathop{\textstyle\bigoplus}\limits_{k \in \mathbf{N}}H^i(X, {\mathscr{J}}^k F), \quad i \in \mathbf{Z}, \]

where \(H^i\) is endowed with the structure of graded \(S\)-module defined by 1.11, where one has taken \(U=X'\).

The proof is modeled on that of EGA III 4.1.5; let us give a summary.

One works on \(\varphi_i\) and \(\psi_i\), to which correspond homomorphisms of modules:

\[ \tag{1.16} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{ & H^i(\widehat{X}, \widehat{F}) \ar[d]^-{\psi_i} \\ H^i(X, F)^{\wedge}\ar[r]^-{\varphi_i}& \varprojlim_k H^i(X, F_k). } \end{array} \]

(a) One supposes only that \(H^i\) is a graded \(S\)-module of finite type. One deduces from this that the filtration defined on \(H^i(X, F)\) by the modules:

\[ \tag{1.17} R^i_k=\ker(H^i(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i(X, F_k)) \]

is \(I\)-good. For this one uses the exact cohomology sequence:

\[ \tag{1.18} H^i(X, {\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i(X, F_k), \]

which proves that the graded \(S\)-module \(\coprod_{k \in \mathbf{N}} R_k^i\) is a quotient of the graded \(S\)-submodule

\[ \coprod_{k \in \mathbf{N}}H^i(X, {\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}F) \]

of \(H^i\), hence is of finite type, since \(S\) is noetherian. Whence this first point.

Set:

\[ \tag{1.19} M^i=H^i(X, F), \quad H^i_k=H^i(X, F_k). \]

One has a commutative diagram:

\[ \tag{1.20} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix@R=6mm{ H^i(X, F)^{\wedge} \ar[r]^-{s_i}\ar[dr]_{\varphi_i} &\varprojlim_{k}(M^i/R^i_k)\ar[d]^{t_i} \\ &\varprojlim_{k}H^i_k\,, } \end{array} \]

in which \(s_i\) is an isomorphism; indeed the filtration of \(H^i(X, F)\) is \(I\)-good. Moreover \(t_i\) is a monomorphism; indeed the functor \(\varprojlim\) is left exact, and, for every \(k\geqslant 0\), the natural morphism \(M^i/R^i_k \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i_k\) is a monomorphism, by definition of \(R^i_k\).

To study the surjectivity of \(t_i\) one introduces:

\[ \tag{1.21} Q_k^i = \operatorname{coker} (H^i(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i(X, F_k)), \]

whence a projective system of exact sequences:

\[ \tag{1.22} 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R_k^i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M^i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i_k \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Q_k^i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0. \]

Using the exact cohomology sequence:

\[ \tag{1.23} H^i(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i(X, F_k)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^{i+1}(X, {\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}F), \]

one sees that the \(S\)-module graded

\[ \tag{1.24} Q^i=\coprod_{k \in \mathbf{N}}Q^i_k \]

is a graded \(S\)-submodule of \(H^{i+1}\). Moreover, for every \(k\geqslant 0\), one has:

\[ \tag{1.25} I^{k+1}Q^i_k=0 \]

since \(Q_k^i\) is the image of \(H^i_k\).

(b) One supposes only that \(H^{i+1}\) is of finite type and one is interested in \(t_i\) (forgetting \(s_i\)). Since \(S\) is noetherian, \(Q^i\) is of finite type; applying a classical lemma, one finds that there exist an integer \(r\geqslant 0\) and an integer \(k_0\geqslant 0\) such that

\[ \tag{1.26} I^rQ_k^i=0 \quad \text{for } k\geqslant k_0. \]

It follows that the projective system \((Q_k^i)_{k\in \mathbf{N}}\) is essentially zero, hence that the projective system \((H_k^i)_{k\in \mathbf{N}}\) satisfies the uniform Mittag-Leffler condition. From the exact sequence (1.22) one deduces the exact sequence

\[ \tag{1.27} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M^i/R_k^i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i_k \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Q_k^i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

whence the exact sequence:

\[ \tag{1.28} 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\varprojlim_{k}M^i/R_k^i \xrightarrow{t_i} \varprojlim_{k}H^i_k \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varprojlim_{k} Q_k^i. \]

Now the projective system \((Q_k^i)_{k\in \mathbf{N}}\) is essentially zero, hence \(t_i\) is an isomorphism.

(c) Let us prove that, if \(H^i\) is of finite type, \(\psi_i\) is an isomorphism. It suffices to apply \(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{III}}\) 13.3.1 taking as a base of open sets of \(X\) the affine open sets. This is legitimate; indeed, by (b), the projective system \((H_k^{i-1})_{k\in \mathbf{N}}\) satisfies the Mittag-Leffler condition.

The theorem follows formally from (a), (b) and (c). One will note that in fact the proof uses, at each step, the finiteness of \(H^i\) only for a single value of \(i\).

Let us give some examples where the hypothesis of theorem 1.1 is satisfied.

Corollary 1.2

Suppose that \(\mathscr{I}\) is generated by a section \(t'\) of \(\mathscr{O}_{X'}\), and denote by \(t\) the corresponding section of \(\mathscr{O}_X\). Let \(F\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-module and let \(n\) be an integer.

Suppose that:

  1. \(t\) is \(F\)-regular (i.e. the homothety \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} tX\) in \(F\) is a monomorphism).

  2. \(R^if_*(F)\) is coherent for \(i=n-1\) and \(i=n\).

Then the hypothesis of theorem 1.1 is satisfied.

Indeed, one notes that it defines an isomorphism \(\mathscr{J}^kF\simeq\mathscr{J} F\) and one deduces from this that

\[ \tag{1.29} \mathscr{H}^i \simeq R^if_*(F)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{X'}}\mathscr{O}_{X'}[T], \]

where \(T\) is an indeterminate. Whence the conclusion.

Corollary 1.3

Suppose that \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), where \(A\) is a noetherian ring that is separated and complete for the \(I\)-adic topology. Suppose that the \(S\)-module \(H^i\) is of finite type for \(i=n-1\) and \(i=n\). (cf. 1.14 and 1.15). Then the hypotheses of theorem 1.1 are satisfied and one finds a commutative diagram of isomorphisms:

\[ \tag{1.1} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{ H^i(X, F)\ar[rr]^{\rho'_i}\ar[dr]_{\varphi'_i} && H^i(\widehat{X}, \widehat{F}) \ar[dl]^{\psi_i}& \\ &\varprojlim_{k}H^i(X, F_k)&& \text{for } i=n-1.} \end{array} \]

One simply notes that \(H^i(X, F)\) is of finite type hence isomorphic to its completion. One obtains (1.1) by transcribing into the category of \(A\)-modules the diagram of Modules (1.7), and by replacing the left-hand vertical by \(H^i(X, F)\).

Proposition 1.4

Let \(A\) be a noetherian ring. Let \(t\in A\) and suppose that \(A\) is separated and complete for the \((tA)\)-adic topology. Set:

\[ \tag{1.31} X'=\operatorname{Spec} (A), \quad Y'=V(t), \quad I=(tA). \]

Let \(T\) be a closed subset of \(X'\), set

\[ \tag{1.32} X=X'-T, \quad Y=Y'\cap X=Y'-(Y'\cap T). \]

Let \(F\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Finally let

\[ \tag{1.33} T'= \{ x\in X' \mid \operatorname{codim}(\{ \overline{x}\} \cap T, \{\overline{x}\})=1\}. \]

Suppose that:

  1. \(t\) is \(F\)-regular,

  2. \(\operatorname{prof}_{T'}(F) \geqslant n+1\),

  3. \(A\) is a quotient of a regular noetherian ring.

Then, in the diagram (1.1), the morphisms \(\rho'_i\), \(\varphi'_i\) and \(\psi_i\) are isomorphisms for \(i<n\) and monomorphisms for \(i=n\). Moreover \(\psi_n\) is an isomorphism.

By virtue of 1.3 and 1.2, it suffices to prove that \(R^if_*(F)\) is coherent for \(i\leqslant n\), which follows from the finiteness theorem 2.1.

In particular:

Example 1.5

One will apply 1.4 when \(A\) is a local ring and \(t\) belongs to the radical \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\) of \(A\). One will then take \(T=\{ \mathfrak{r}(A)\}\). In this case, for \(n=1\) one finds the following statement:

If \(A\) is separated and complete for the \(t\)-adic topology and a quotient of a regular ring (for example if \(A\) is complete), if moreover \(t\) is \(F\)-regular and if \(\operatorname{prof} F_x\geqslant 2\) for every \(x\in \operatorname{Spec} (A)\) such that \(\dim A/x=1\), then the natural homomorphism

\[ F(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma(X, F) \]

is an isomorphism.

Indeed, keeping the notations of 1.4, one has \(T=\{ \mathfrak{r}(A)\}\) and formula (1.33) says that

\[ T'=\{x\in\operatorname{Spec}(A)\mid\dim A/x=1\}. \]

blocks.html: 1 The comparison theorem

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X'\) be a morphism of preschemes, separated and of finite type. Suppose that \(X'\) is locally noetherian. Let \(Y'\) be a closed subset of \(X'\) and let \(Y\subset f^{-1}(Y')\). Let \(\widehat{X}\) and \(\widehat{X'}\) be the formal completions of \(X\) and of \(X'\) along \(Y\) and \(Y'\). Let \(\widehat{f}\) be the morphism deduced from \(f\) by passage to the completions.
\[ \tag{1.1} \xymatrix{ X \ar[d]_f & Y \ar[l] \ar[d]& &\widehat{X}\ar[d]_{\widehat{f}} \ar[r]^j & X\ar[d]^f &\\ X^{\prime} & Y^{\prime} \ar[l] &, &\widehat{X^{\prime}} \ar[r]^i & X^{\prime}. } \]
We denote by \(j\) (resp. \(i\)) the homomorphism from \(\widehat{X}\) into \(X\) (resp. from \(\widehat{X'}\) into \(X'\)). It is known that \(i\) and \(j\) are flat.
Let \(\mathscr{I} '\) be an ideal of definition of \(Y'\) and let \({\mathscr{J}}=f^{*}(\mathscr{I} '). \mathscr{O}_X\), it is an ideal of definition of \(Y\). We thus have:
\[ \tag{1.2} {\widehat{X'}=(Y', \varprojlim_{k \in \mathbf{N}}\mathscr{O}_{X'}/{{\mathscr{I}'}^{k+1}})}, \quad {\widehat{X}=(Y, \varprojlim_{k \in \mathbf{N}}\mathscr{O}_X/{{\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}})}. \]
For every \(k \in \mathbf{N}\), set:
\[ \tag{1.3} Y'_k=(Y', \mathscr{O}_{X'}/{{\mathscr{I}'}^{k+1}}), \quad {Y_k}=(Y, \mathscr{O}_X/{{\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}}). \]
Let \(F\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. For every \(k \in \mathbf{N}\), one sets:
\[ \tag{1.4} F_k=F/{\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}F, \qquad \widehat{F}=j^*(F)\simeq \varprojlim F_k. \]
If one sets:
\[ \tag{1.5} R^i f_*(F)^{\wedge}=\varprojlim_{k \in \mathbf{N}} (R^i f_*(F)^{\wedge} \otimes_{{\mathscr{O}}_{X'}}{\mathscr{O}}_{Y'_k}), \quad i \in \mathbf{Z}, \]
one has a natural homomorphism:
\[ \tag{1.6} r_i: i^*(R^if_*(F)) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R^i f_*(F)^{\wedge}, \]
which is an isomorphism when \( R^i f_*(F)\) is coherent.
As is explained in EGA III 4.1.1, one has a commutative diagram:

\[ \tag{1.7} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{ i^*(R^i f_*(F)) \ar[rr]^-{\rho_i} \ar[d]^{r_i} & &R^i\widehat{f}_*(\widehat{F}) \ar[d]^{\psi_i} \\ R^i f_*(F)^{\wedge}\ar[rr]^-{\varphi_i} & &\varprojlim_{k \in \mathbf{N}} R^i f_*(F_k). } \end{array} \]

In loc. cit. one finds a commutative diagram, for it is known that \( R^if_*(F)\) is coherent, and one identifies the source and the target of (1.6). In our case, \( R^if_*(F)\) will be coherent only for certain values of \(i\), for which one will study (1.7).
Consider the graded ring
\[ \tag{1.8} \mathscr{S} = \coprod_{k \in \mathbf{N}} \mathscr{I}'^k, \]
and the graded \(\mathscr{S}\)-Module:
\[ \tag{1.9} \mathscr{H}^i= \coprod_{k \in \mathbf{N}} R^if_*({\mathscr{J}}^k F), \quad i \in \mathbf{Z}, \]
whose structure of \(\mathscr{S}\)-Module is defined as follows.
The sheaf \(R^if_*({\mathscr{J}}^k F)\) is associated with the presheaf which, to every affine open set \(U\) of \(X'\), associates:
\[ \tag{1.10} H^i(f^{-1}(U), {\mathscr{J}}^k F{|f^{-1}(U)}). \]
So let \(U'\) be an affine open set of \(X'\), set
\[ U=f^{-1}(U'), \]

and let \(x\in {\mathscr{I}}^m(U')\). Let \(x'\) be the image of \(x\) in \({\mathscr{J}}^{{k}} (U)\). The homothety of ratio \(x'\) in \(F_{|U'}\) maps \({\mathscr{J}}^kF{{|U'}}\) into \({\mathscr{J}}^{k+m}F{{|U'}}\), whence, by functoriality, a morphism:
\[ \tag{1.11} \mu^i_{x, k}(U):H^i(U', {\mathscr{J}}^kF{{|U'}})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i(U', {\mathscr{J}}^{k+m}F{{|U'}}), \]
defined for every \(i \in \mathbf{Z}\) and every \(k \in \mathbf{N}\), which gives, by passage to the associated sheaf, the structure of graded \(\mathscr{S}\)-Module of \(\mathscr{H}^i\).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 1.1

Let \(n\) be an integer. Suppose that the graded \(\mathscr{S}\)-Module \(\mathscr{H}^i\) is of finite type for \(i=n-1\) and \(i=n\), then:

  1. \(r_n\) and \(r_{n-1}\) are isomorphisms and \(R^i\widehat{f}_*(\widehat{F})\) is coherent for \(i=n-1\);

  2. for \(i=n-1\), \(\rho_i\), \(\varphi_i\) and \(\psi_i\) are topological isomorphisms (in particular the filtration defined on \(R^{n-1}f_*(F)\) by the kernels of the homomorphisms

    \[ \tag{1.12} R^{n-1}f_*(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R^{n-1}f_*(F_k) \]

    is \(\mathscr{I}'\)-good);

  3. for \(i=n\), \(\rho_i\), \(\varphi_i\) and \(\psi_i\) are monomorphisms, moreover the filtration on \(R^n f_*(F)\) is \(\mathscr{I}'\)-good and \(\psi_n\) is an isomorphism;

  4. The projective system of the \(R^i f_*(F_k)\) satisfies, for \(i=n-2, n-1\), the uniform Mittag-Leffler condition, i.e. there exists a fixed integer \(k\geqslant 0\) such that, for every \(p\geqslant 0\) and every \(p'\geqslant p+k\), one has:

    \[ \operatorname{Im}[R^i f_*(F_{p'})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R^i f_*(F_p)]=\operatorname{Im}[R^i f_*(F_{p+k})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R^i f_*(F_p)]. \]
By proceeding as in EGA III 4.1.8, it is easy to reduce to the case where \(X'\) is the spectrum of a noetherian ring \(A\). In this case, it is known that
\[ \tag{1.13} R^if_*(F)=H^i(X, F)^\thicksim \quad \text{(cf. 1.10).} \]
Let \(I\) be the ideal of \(A\) such that \(\widetilde{I}=\mathscr{I}'\) and let
\[ \tag{1.14} S=\mathop{\textstyle\bigoplus}\limits_{k \in \mathbf{N}}I^k, \]
\[ \tag{1.15} H^i=\mathop{\textstyle\bigoplus}\limits_{k \in \mathbf{N}}H^i(X, {\mathscr{J}}^k F), \quad i \in \mathbf{Z}, \]
where \(H^i\) is endowed with the structure of graded \(S\)-module defined by 1.11, where one has taken \(U=X'\).
The proof is modeled on that of EGA III 4.1.5; let us give a summary.
One works on \(\varphi_i\) and \(\psi_i\), to which correspond homomorphisms of modules:
\[ \tag{1.16} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{ & H^i(\widehat{X}, \widehat{F}) \ar[d]^-{\psi_i} \\ H^i(X, F)^{\wedge}\ar[r]^-{\varphi_i}& \varprojlim_k H^i(X, F_k). } \end{array} \]
(a) One supposes only that \(H^i\) is a graded \(S\)-module of finite type. One deduces from this that the filtration defined on \(H^i(X, F)\) by the modules:
\[ \tag{1.17} R^i_k=\ker(H^i(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i(X, F_k)) \]
is \(I\)-good. For this one uses the exact cohomology sequence:

\[ \tag{1.18} H^i(X, {\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i(X, F_k), \]
which proves that the graded \(S\)-module \(\coprod_{k \in \mathbf{N}} R_k^i\) is a quotient of the graded \(S\)-submodule
\[ \coprod_{k \in \mathbf{N}}H^i(X, {\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}F) \]
of \(H^i\), hence is of finite type, since \(S\) is noetherian. Whence this first point.
Set:
\[ \tag{1.19} M^i=H^i(X, F), \quad H^i_k=H^i(X, F_k). \]
One has a commutative diagram:
\[ \tag{1.20} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix@R=6mm{ H^i(X, F)^{\wedge} \ar[r]^-{s_i}\ar[dr]_{\varphi_i} &\varprojlim_{k}(M^i/R^i_k)\ar[d]^{t_i} \\ &\varprojlim_{k}H^i_k\,, } \end{array} \]

in which \(s_i\) is an isomorphism; indeed the filtration of \(H^i(X, F)\) is \(I\)-good. Moreover \(t_i\) is a monomorphism; indeed the functor \(\varprojlim\) is left exact, and, for every \(k\geqslant 0\), the natural morphism \(M^i/R^i_k \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i_k\) is a monomorphism, by definition of \(R^i_k\).
To study the surjectivity of \(t_i\) one introduces:
\[ \tag{1.21} Q_k^i = \operatorname{coker} (H^i(X, F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i(X, F_k)), \]
whence a projective system of exact sequences:

\[ \tag{1.22} 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} R_k^i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M^i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i_k \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Q_k^i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0. \]
Using the exact cohomology sequence:

\[ \tag{1.23} H^i(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i(X, F_k)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^{i+1}(X, {\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}F), \]
one sees that the \(S\)-module graded
\[ \tag{1.24} Q^i=\coprod_{k \in \mathbf{N}}Q^i_k \]
is a graded \(S\)-submodule of \(H^{i+1}\). Moreover, for every \(k\geqslant 0\), one has:
\[ \tag{1.25} I^{k+1}Q^i_k=0 \]
since \(Q_k^i\) is the image of \(H^i_k\).

(b) One supposes only that \(H^{i+1}\) is of finite type and one is interested in \(t_i\) (forgetting \(s_i\)). Since \(S\) is noetherian, \(Q^i\) is of finite type; applying a classical lemma, one finds that there exist an integer \(r\geqslant 0\) and an integer \(k_0\geqslant 0\) such that
\[ \tag{1.26} I^rQ_k^i=0 \quad \text{for } k\geqslant k_0. \]

It follows that the projective system \((Q_k^i)_{k\in \mathbf{N}}\) is essentially zero, hence that the projective system \((H_k^i)_{k\in \mathbf{N}}\) satisfies the uniform Mittag-Leffler condition. From the exact sequence (1.22) one deduces the exact sequence

\[ \tag{1.27} 0 \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M^i/R_k^i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} H^i_k \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Q_k^i \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]
whence the exact sequence:

\[ \tag{1.28} 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\varprojlim_{k}M^i/R_k^i \xrightarrow{t_i} \varprojlim_{k}H^i_k \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varprojlim_{k} Q_k^i. \]
Now the projective system \((Q_k^i)_{k\in \mathbf{N}}\) is essentially zero, hence \(t_i\) is an isomorphism.

(c) Let us prove that, if \(H^i\) is of finite type, \(\psi_i\) is an isomorphism. It suffices to apply \(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{III}}\) 13.3.1 taking as a base of open sets of \(X\) the affine open sets. This is legitimate; indeed, by (b), the projective system \((H_k^{i-1})_{k\in \mathbf{N}}\) satisfies the Mittag-Leffler condition.
The theorem follows formally from (a), (b) and (c). One will note that in fact the proof uses, at each step, the finiteness of \(H^i\) only for a single value of \(i\).
Let us give some examples where the hypothesis of theorem 1.1 is satisfied.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.2

Suppose that \(\mathscr{I}\) is generated by a section \(t'\) of \(\mathscr{O}_{X'}\), and denote by \(t\) the corresponding section of \(\mathscr{O}_X\). Let \(F\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-module and let \(n\) be an integer.

Suppose that:

  1. \(t\) is \(F\)-regular (i.e. the homothety \(X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} tX\) in \(F\) is a monomorphism).

  2. \(R^if_*(F)\) is coherent for \(i=n-1\) and \(i=n\).

Then the hypothesis of theorem 1.1 is satisfied.
Indeed, one notes that it defines an isomorphism \(\mathscr{J}^kF\simeq\mathscr{J} F\) and one deduces from this that
\[ \tag{1.29} \mathscr{H}^i \simeq R^if_*(F)\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{X'}}\mathscr{O}_{X'}[T], \]
where \(T\) is an indeterminate. Whence the conclusion.

blocks.title: Corollary 1.3
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.3

Suppose that \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), where \(A\) is a noetherian ring that is separated and complete for the \(I\)-adic topology. Suppose that the \(S\)-module \(H^i\) is of finite type for \(i=n-1\) and \(i=n\). (cf. 1.14 and 1.15). Then the hypotheses of theorem 1.1 are satisfied and one finds a commutative diagram of isomorphisms:

\[ \tag{1.1} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix{ H^i(X, F)\ar[rr]^{\rho'_i}\ar[dr]_{\varphi'_i} && H^i(\widehat{X}, \widehat{F}) \ar[dl]^{\psi_i}& \\ &\varprojlim_{k}H^i(X, F_k)&& \text{for } i=n-1.} \end{array} \]

One simply notes that \(H^i(X, F)\) is of finite type hence isomorphic to its completion. One obtains (1.1) by transcribing into the category of \(A\)-modules the diagram of Modules (1.7), and by replacing the left-hand vertical by \(H^i(X, F)\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.4

Let \(A\) be a noetherian ring. Let \(t\in A\) and suppose that \(A\) is separated and complete for the \((tA)\)-adic topology. Set:

\[ \tag{1.31} X'=\operatorname{Spec} (A), \quad Y'=V(t), \quad I=(tA). \]

Let \(T\) be a closed subset of \(X'\), set

\[ \tag{1.32} X=X'-T, \quad Y=Y'\cap X=Y'-(Y'\cap T). \]

Let \(F\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Finally let

\[ \tag{1.33} T'= \{ x\in X' \mid \operatorname{codim}(\{ \overline{x}\} \cap T, \{\overline{x}\})=1\}. \]

Suppose that:

  1. \(t\) is \(F\)-regular,

  2. \(\operatorname{prof}_{T'}(F) \geqslant n+1\),

  3. \(A\) is a quotient of a regular noetherian ring.

Then, in the diagram (1.1), the morphisms \(\rho'_i\), \(\varphi'_i\) and \(\psi_i\) are isomorphisms for \(i<n\) and monomorphisms for \(i=n\). Moreover \(\psi_n\) is an isomorphism.
By virtue of 1.3 and 1.2, it suffices to prove that \(R^if_*(F)\) is coherent for \(i\leqslant n\), which follows from the finiteness theorem 2.1.
In particular:

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 1.5

One will apply 1.4 when \(A\) is a local ring and \(t\) belongs to the radical \(\mathfrak{r}(A)\) of \(A\). One will then take \(T=\{ \mathfrak{r}(A)\}\). In this case, for \(n=1\) one finds the following statement:

If \(A\) is separated and complete for the \(t\)-adic topology and a quotient of a regular ring (for example if \(A\) is complete), if moreover \(t\) is \(F\)-regular and if \(\operatorname{prof} F_x\geqslant 2\) for every \(x\in \operatorname{Spec} (A)\) such that \(\dim A/x=1\), then the natural homomorphism

\[ F(X, F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \Gamma(X, F) \]

is an isomorphism.

Indeed, keeping the notations of 1.4, one has \(T=\{ \mathfrak{r}(A)\}\) and formula (1.33) says that

\[ T'=\{x\in\operatorname{Spec}(A)\mid\dim A/x=1\}. \]

pages.title: Existence theorem
pages.html:
Existence theorem

Let us first state EGA III 3.4.2 in a slightly more general form.

Let \(f:\mathfrak{X} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathfrak{X}'\) be an adic morphism1 of formal preschemes, with \(\mathfrak{X}'\) noetherian. Let \(\mathscr{I}'\) be an ideal of definition of \(\mathfrak{X}'\); since \(f\) is adic, \(f^*\mathscr{I}'=\mathscr{J}\) is an ideal of definition of \(\mathfrak{X}\).

For every \(n\in \mathbf{N}\), set

\[ \tag{2.1} \mathfrak{X}_n=(\mathfrak{X}, \mathscr{O}_\mathfrak{X}/{\mathscr{J}}^{n+1}), \]

this is an ordinary prescheme which has the same underlying topological space as \(\mathfrak{X}\).

Let \(\mathfrak{F}\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_\mathfrak{X}\)-Module. For every \(k\in\mathbf{N}\), the \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathfrak{X}_k}\)-Modules

\[ \tag{2.2} F_k=\mathfrak{F}/{\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}\mathfrak{F} \]

are coherent. For every \(i\), we have a homomorphism

\[ \tag{2.3} \psi_i: R^if_*(\mathfrak{F}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varprojlim_k R^if_*(F_k), \]

deduced by functoriality from the natural homomorphism:

\[ \tag{2.4} \mathfrak{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F_k. \]

Set

\[ \tag{2.5} \mathbf{S}= \operatorname{gr}_{{\mathscr{I}'}}\mathscr{O}_{\mathfrak{X}'}=\coprod_{k \in \mathbf{N}} {\mathscr{I}'}^k/{\mathscr{I}'}^{k+1}, \]
\[ \tag{2.6} \operatorname{gr}_{\mathscr{J}'}(\mathfrak{F})=\coprod_{k \in \mathbf{N}} \mathscr{J}'^k\mathfrak{F}/\mathscr{J}'^{k+1}\mathfrak{F}, \]
\[ \tag{2.7} \mathbf{K}^i=R^if_*(\operatorname{gr}_{{\mathscr{J}}}(\mathfrak{F}))=\coprod_{k \in \mathbf{N}}R^if_* ({\mathscr{J}}^k \mathfrak{F}/{\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}\mathfrak{F}\;). \]

It is clear that \(\mathbf{K}^i\) is endowed with a structure of graded \(\mathbf{S}\)-Module.

Theorem 2.1

Suppose that \(\mathbf{K}^i\) is a graded \(\mathbf{S}\)-Module of finite type for \(i=n-1\), \(i=n\), \(i=n+1\); then:

  1. \(R^nf_*(\mathfrak{F})\) is coherent.

  2. The homomorphism \(\psi_n\) (2.3) is a topological isomorphism. The natural filtration of the right-hand side of (2.3) is \({\mathscr{J}}\)-good.

  3. The projective system of the \(R^nf_*(F_k)\) satisfies the uniform Mittag-Leffler condition.

The proof is very easy starting from \(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{III}} 13.7.7\) (cf. EGA III \(3.4.2\)), provided one corrects the text on page 78 as follows2

Delete

  1. line 6

    "and \((R^{n+1}T(A_k))_{k\in \mathbf{Z}}\)"

  2. line 13

    "and \(p+q=n+1\)"

  3. line 23

    "and for \(n+1\)".

Theorem 2.2

Let \(A\) be a noetherian adic ring and let \(I\) be an ideal of definition of \(A\). Let \(T\) be a closed subset of \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). Suppose that \(I\) is generated by a \(t \in A\). Let us resume the notations 1.31, 1.32 and 1.33. Let \(\mathfrak{F}\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\)-Module. Set

\[ \tag{2.1} F_0=\mathfrak{F} / {\mathscr{J}} \mathfrak{F}, \]

where \({\mathscr{J}}=t \mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\) is an ideal of definition of \(\widehat{X}\). Suppose that \(A\) is a quotient of a regular noetherian ring and that:

  1. \(t\) is \(\mathfrak{F}\)-regular,

  2. \(\operatorname{prof}_{T'}\mathfrak{F}_0\geqslant 2\).

Then there exists a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\) such that \(\widehat{\mathfrak{F}}\simeq \mathfrak{F}\).

It suffices to prove that \(\widehat{f}_*(\mathfrak{F})\) is a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\)-Module, where \(\widehat{f}: \widehat{X} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widehat{X'}\) is the morphism of formal preschemes deduced from the injection of \(X\) into \(X'\) by completion with respect to \(t\). Indeed, \(A\) is separated and complete for the \(t\)-adic topology, so there will exist an \(A\)-module \(F'\) whose completion will be isomorphic to \(\widehat{f}_*(\mathfrak{F})\). Since \(X\) is an open set of \(X'\), one may take \(F=\widetilde{F'}_{|X}\).

It remains to show that 2.1 is applicable to the morphism of formal preschemes \(\widehat{f}\) and to \(\mathfrak{F}\). Now, by hypothesis (1), for every \(k\in \mathbf{N}\) we have an isomorphism:

\[ {\mathscr{J}}^k \mathfrak{F} /{\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}\mathfrak{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathfrak{F} / {\mathscr{J}} \mathfrak{F}, \]

whence it follows that the hypothesis of 2.1 will be satisfied if one knows that

\[ R^if_*(F_0)\text{ is coherent for }i \leqslant 1. \]

Now this follows from (2) and the finiteness theorem 2.1. Whence the conclusion.

It remains to specialize this statement by supposing that \(A\) is a local ring.

Corollary 2.3

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring and let \(t \in \mathfrak{r}(A)\), an element of the radical of \(A\). Suppose that \(A\) is separated and complete for the \(t\)-adic topology, and, moreover, a quotient of a regular ring (for example, suppose that \(A\) is a complete noetherian local ring). Set

\[ \tag{2.9} X'=\operatorname{Spec} A, \quad T = \{ \mathfrak{r}(A) \}, \]

and let us resume the notations (1.31), (1.32) and (1.33). Let \(\mathfrak{F}\) be an \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\)-Module. Suppose that:

  1. \(t\) is \(\mathfrak{F}\)-regular,

  2. \(\operatorname{prof}_{T'} \mathfrak{F}_0 \geqslant 2\), with \(\mathfrak{F}_0=\mathfrak{F}/{\mathscr{J}}\mathfrak{F}\) and \({\mathscr{J}}=t\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\).

    Then there exists a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\) such that \(\widehat{F}\simeq \mathfrak{F}\).

Let us remark that here \(T'\) is the set of prime ideals \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\) such that \(\dim A/\mathfrak{p} =1\).

blocks.html: Existence theorem
Let us first state EGA III 3.4.2 in a slightly more general form.

Let \(f:\mathfrak{X} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathfrak{X}'\) be an adic morphism1 of formal preschemes, with \(\mathfrak{X}'\) noetherian. Let \(\mathscr{I}'\) be an ideal of definition of \(\mathfrak{X}'\); since \(f\) is adic, \(f^*\mathscr{I}'=\mathscr{J}\) is an ideal of definition of \(\mathfrak{X}\).
For every \(n\in \mathbf{N}\), set
\[ \tag{2.1} \mathfrak{X}_n=(\mathfrak{X}, \mathscr{O}_\mathfrak{X}/{\mathscr{J}}^{n+1}), \]
this is an ordinary prescheme which has the same underlying topological space as \(\mathfrak{X}\).
Let \(\mathfrak{F}\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_\mathfrak{X}\)-Module. For every \(k\in\mathbf{N}\), the \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathfrak{X}_k}\)-Modules
\[ \tag{2.2} F_k=\mathfrak{F}/{\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}\mathfrak{F} \]
are coherent. For every \(i\), we have a homomorphism
\[ \tag{2.3} \psi_i: R^if_*(\mathfrak{F}) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \varprojlim_k R^if_*(F_k), \]
deduced by functoriality from the natural homomorphism:
\[ \tag{2.4} \mathfrak{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F_k. \]
Set
\[ \tag{2.5} \mathbf{S}= \operatorname{gr}_{{\mathscr{I}'}}\mathscr{O}_{\mathfrak{X}'}=\coprod_{k \in \mathbf{N}} {\mathscr{I}'}^k/{\mathscr{I}'}^{k+1}, \]
\[ \tag{2.6} \operatorname{gr}_{\mathscr{J}'}(\mathfrak{F})=\coprod_{k \in \mathbf{N}} \mathscr{J}'^k\mathfrak{F}/\mathscr{J}'^{k+1}\mathfrak{F}, \]
\[ \tag{2.7} \mathbf{K}^i=R^if_*(\operatorname{gr}_{{\mathscr{J}}}(\mathfrak{F}))=\coprod_{k \in \mathbf{N}}R^if_* ({\mathscr{J}}^k \mathfrak{F}/{\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}\mathfrak{F}\;). \]
It is clear that \(\mathbf{K}^i\) is endowed with a structure of graded \(\mathbf{S}\)-Module.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.1

Suppose that \(\mathbf{K}^i\) is a graded \(\mathbf{S}\)-Module of finite type for \(i=n-1\), \(i=n\), \(i=n+1\); then:

  1. \(R^nf_*(\mathfrak{F})\) is coherent.

  2. The homomorphism \(\psi_n\) (2.3) is a topological isomorphism. The natural filtration of the right-hand side of (2.3) is \({\mathscr{J}}\)-good.

  3. The projective system of the \(R^nf_*(F_k)\) satisfies the uniform Mittag-Leffler condition.
The proof is very easy starting from \(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{III}} 13.7.7\) (cf. EGA III \(3.4.2\)), provided one corrects the text on page 78 as follows2
Delete

1. line 6

    "and \((R^{n+1}T(A_k))_{k\in \mathbf{Z}}\)"

  2. line 13

    "and \(p+q=n+1\)"

  3. line 23

    "and for \(n+1\)".

blocks.title: Theorem 2.2
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.2

Let \(A\) be a noetherian adic ring and let \(I\) be an ideal of definition of \(A\). Let \(T\) be a closed subset of \(X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). Suppose that \(I\) is generated by a \(t \in A\). Let us resume the notations 1.31, 1.32 and 1.33. Let \(\mathfrak{F}\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\)-Module. Set

\[ \tag{2.1} F_0=\mathfrak{F} / {\mathscr{J}} \mathfrak{F}, \]

where \({\mathscr{J}}=t \mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\) is an ideal of definition of \(\widehat{X}\). Suppose that \(A\) is a quotient of a regular noetherian ring and that:

  1. \(t\) is \(\mathfrak{F}\)-regular,

  2. \(\operatorname{prof}_{T'}\mathfrak{F}_0\geqslant 2\).

Then there exists a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\) such that \(\widehat{\mathfrak{F}}\simeq \mathfrak{F}\).

It suffices to prove that \(\widehat{f}_*(\mathfrak{F})\) is a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\)-Module, where \(\widehat{f}: \widehat{X} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \widehat{X'}\) is the morphism of formal preschemes deduced from the injection of \(X\) into \(X'\) by completion with respect to \(t\). Indeed, \(A\) is separated and complete for the \(t\)-adic topology, so there will exist an \(A\)-module \(F'\) whose completion will be isomorphic to \(\widehat{f}_*(\mathfrak{F})\). Since \(X\) is an open set of \(X'\), one may take \(F=\widetilde{F'}_{|X}\).
It remains to show that 2.1 is applicable to the morphism of formal preschemes \(\widehat{f}\) and to \(\mathfrak{F}\). Now, by hypothesis (1), for every \(k\in \mathbf{N}\) we have an isomorphism:
\[ {\mathscr{J}}^k \mathfrak{F} /{\mathscr{J}}^{k+1}\mathfrak{F} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathfrak{F} / {\mathscr{J}} \mathfrak{F}, \]
whence it follows that the hypothesis of 2.1 will be satisfied if one knows that
\[ R^if_*(F_0)\text{ is coherent for }i \leqslant 1. \]
Now this follows from (2) and the finiteness theorem 2.1. Whence the conclusion.
It remains to specialize this statement by supposing that \(A\) is a local ring.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3

Let \(A\) be a noetherian local ring and let \(t \in \mathfrak{r}(A)\), an element of the radical of \(A\). Suppose that \(A\) is separated and complete for the \(t\)-adic topology, and, moreover, a quotient of a regular ring (for example, suppose that \(A\) is a complete noetherian local ring). Set

\[ \tag{2.9} X'=\operatorname{Spec} A, \quad T = \{ \mathfrak{r}(A) \}, \]

and let us resume the notations (1.31), (1.32) and (1.33). Let \(\mathfrak{F}\) be an \(\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\)-Module. Suppose that:

  1. \(t\) is \(\mathfrak{F}\)-regular,

  2. \(\operatorname{prof}_{T'} \mathfrak{F}_0 \geqslant 2\), with \(\mathfrak{F}_0=\mathfrak{F}/{\mathscr{J}}\mathfrak{F}\) and \({\mathscr{J}}=t\mathscr{O}_{\widehat{X}}\).

    Then there exists a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \(F\) such that \(\widehat{F}\simeq \mathfrak{F}\).
Let us remark that here \(T'\) is the set of prime ideals \(\mathfrak{p}\) of \(A\) such that \(\dim A/\mathfrak{p} =1\).

footnotes.html: This hypothesis is not essential, cf. XII, p. .
